briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. We will now begin the Climate Change Daily Briefing for May 26, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Intensification of Global Extreme Weather Phenomena and Immediate Threats
sections.content: News collected today indicates a severe escalation of the global climate crisis. In Europe and the UK, early heatwaves are becoming commonplace, with May temperatures exceeding 34 degrees Celsius for the first time ever and record-breaking tropical nights being experienced [2, 18, 123, 144, 146]. Extreme heatwaves are threatening the lives of people worldwide, as evidenced by gig workers in India suffering from exhaustion in the scorching heat [3, 148]. Domestically, direct impacts of climate change are also evident, including the acceleration of pine pollen season, increased concerns about hail, and the early arrival of food poisoning season [4, 5, 7]. Furthermore, it has been confirmed that global ecosystem disturbances are intensifying, with a surge in flood and erosion risks in the Himalayan rivers, known as the 'water tower for 2 billion people,' changes in Arctic Ocean sea passages leading to fluctuations in ice age cycles, and even the 'Doomsday Glacier' facing crisis [1, 17, 142]. Phenomena such as the weakening of lakes' natural purification functions and Europe's shortened spring warn that climate change is disrupting the fundamental balance of our environment [128, 135, 140, 141].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Efforts for Climate Change Adaptation and Carbon Neutrality Implementation
sections.content: In response to these threats of climate change, various adaptation and carbon neutrality efforts are actively underway domestically. Hwaseong City is initiating a tidal flat ecological environment survey and piloting a fruit tree pollen bank to counter abnormal weather, aiming to stabilize agricultural production [6, 10, 16, 19]. The Korea Rural Community Corporation is dedicating full efforts to prevent floods and droughts through customized water facility maintenance, and local governments like Dongdaemun-gu are implementing comprehensive measures to prepare for heatwaves and floods, thereby strengthening climate crisis adaptation capabilities [15, 20, 21]. Notably, Seongdong-gu achieved 93.2% of its greenhouse gas reduction target last year, receiving excellent evaluations for its implementation of carbon neutrality and climate crisis adaptation measures [64, 65, 66, 68, 80, 82]. Jeonju City is accelerating the establishment of a carbon-neutral city by expanding support for renewable energy installations [23, 44, 45, 57, 73, 94, 99, 112]. Technology-based solutions are also being explored, such as the climate and disaster response cooperation between K-water and OpenAI [11, 13], the emergence of Carbon Capture and Utilization (CCU) technology in the cement industry [29], and the groundbreaking of a hydrogen power plant in Gyeongju City [72]. Furthermore, movements encouraging citizens' participation in daily carbon neutrality practices are spreading nationwide, including Nonsan City's Carbon Neutrality Point System [14, 46, 47, 59, 61, 78, 84], Hana Financial's step donation campaign [43, 51, 54, 62, 69, 74], and the Korea Environment Corporation's Carbon Neutrality Running Campaign [31, 38, 41, 49, 70, 77].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Current Challenges
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy has established itself as a core driver of global climate action, and its growth is remarkable both domestically and internationally. Renewable energy is emerging as a major source of supply in the electricity market, with solar and wind power generation surpassing gas power generation for the first time ever [98]. In Australia, positive effects have also been observed, with household electricity bills decreasing due to the expansion of renewable energy [91]. Domestically, Bonghwa-gun is recognized as a role model for energy-independent cities, recording the highest level of new and renewable energy dissemination nationwide [92, 97, 102, 109]. Companies are expanding their adoption of renewable energy, with Sungshin Cement pursuing a 2-megawatt direct solar PPA (Power Purchase Agreement) to strengthen ESG management and carbon neutrality responses [27, 83, 86, 89, 95, 96, 113, 116, 119, 122]. Furthermore, technological and institutional solutions are being explored, such as transforming the crisis of solar output control into an opportunity through a quasi-central dispatch system for VPPs (Virtual Power Plants) [87, 106, 121]. However, numerous policy and economic challenges remain to be addressed, requiring a balanced approach. These include the 'policy misalignment' between the 100GW renewable energy target and strengthened solar site regulations [101], skepticism regarding the realization of unit cost reductions [105], and the direct impact on offshore wind development due to the abolition of REC (Renewable Energy Certificate) weighting [117].

sections.title: 🌍 Complex Aspects of International Climate Policy and Perception
sections.content: The international community's perception and policy discussions on climate change remain complex and multifaceted. Author Ian McEwan pointed out that pessimism might be a greater problem than climate change itself, emphasizing the importance of proactive responses [125, 145]. Meanwhile, political debates continue, with warnings about the emergence of politicians advocating 'climate denial' and Al Gore stressing the importance of sustained climate action [12]. Particularly, a report indicating that China's revised carbon emission calculation method conceals actual increases and leaves a gap the size of Germany [127, 149] raises transparency issues regarding the achievement of international climate goals. Furthermore, the dilemma between being an AI powerhouse and achieving carbon neutrality [25], or voices cautioning against renewable energy absolutism and advocating for an energy mix that includes nuclear power [103], demonstrate the importance of developing complex, realistic strategies. A report that U.S. immigration enforcement flights generate hundreds of thousands of tons of carbon emissions causing climate damage [143] suggests that climate change is connected to various policy areas beyond mere energy transition. Discussions that setting climate adaptation targets will be the UK's next big step [138] highlight the importance of climate adaptation faced by each nation.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the severity of the global climate crisis, along with various domestic and international response efforts. Through continued attention and cooperation, we must overcome the climate crisis and build a sustainable future. This concludes today's climate briefing.
